title: ARIA Roles & Attributes
summary: ARIA adds accessibility information when HTML alone is not enough.
It can describe roles and states to screen readers.
The first rule is to use real HTML elements when you can.
ARIA is a supplement, not a replacement.

sections.text: Sometimes plain HTML cannot fully describe a custom widget to a screen reader. ARIA is a set of extra attributes that fill that gap, telling assistive tools what something is and what state it is in. But it comes with one big rule.

sections.title: What ARIA Does
sections.text: ARIA, short for Accessible Rich Internet Applications, lets you add information for assistive tools. You can give an element a role, saying it acts like a button or a dialog, and describe its state, like expanded or selected. Screen readers use this to announce the element correctly.

sections.title: The First Rule of ARIA
sections.text: The most important rule is to prefer real HTML elements first. A real button is already accessible. Rebuilding one from a plain box and patching it with ARIA is harder and easier to get wrong. ARIA is a supplement for when no native element fits, not a replacement for using the right tag.

media.alt: A custom widget given a role and state for screen readers

sections.title: Labels on a Custom Built Machine
sections.text: Standard appliances come with built in, understood controls. A custom built machine needs you to add clear labels so anyone can operate it. ARIA is those added labels. But if a standard appliance already does the job, you use that, rather than building a custom one and labeling it from scratch.

media.alt: A standard labelled appliance beside a custom machine needing added labels

sections.title: Using ARIA Wisely
items: First, try to use a real HTML element.
If a native element fits, you likely need no ARIA.
For a truly custom widget, add a role.
Describe its state, like expanded or selected.
Treat ARIA as a supplement, not a replacement.

sections.text: There is a saying among accessibility experts that no ARIA is better than bad ARIA. Incorrect ARIA can confuse screen readers more than having none at all, which is why the use real HTML rule comes first.

terms.definition: Extra attributes that add accessibility info.
What an element acts like, such as a button.
An addition, used only when needed.
A real HTML element to prefer first.

options: Accessibility information when HTML alone is not enough
questions.explanation: ARIA adds accessibility information when plain HTML cannot describe something.

options: Use real HTML elements when you can
questions.explanation: The first rule is to prefer real HTML elements, which are already accessible.

questions.text: Is ARIA a replacement for HTML?
questions.explanation: ARIA is a supplement for when no native element fits, not a replacement.
